title: On the Gamma conjecture associated with toric flips
speaker: Hiroshi Iritani
tags: Homological Mirror Symmetry: Methods and Structures

proof_stages.label: Gamma structure and Stokes compatibility
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the Dubrovin quantum connection, constructs the Gamma integral/rational structure from K-theory using the Gamma class and modified Chern character, and states the Gamma conjecture as compatibility of this structure with the Stokes structure of the irregular singularity at z=0.

proof_stages.label: Toric mirror and log large-radius compactification
proof_stages.summary: Using the basic toric mirror picture, the quantum cohomology and quantum connection are identified with the Jacobian ring and twisted de Rham/Saito structure of the mirror potential, then partially compactified over the large-radius divisor q=0 via logarithmic geometry so that the log Jacobian remains flat and limits to classical cohomology.

proof_stages.label: General secondary-fan compactification
proof_stages.summary: The general toric-stack input is encoded by the exact sequence L -> Z^S -> N, its Hori-Vafa mirror is constructed on the torus with potential W=sum u_b, and the GKZ/secondary-fan compactification supplies affine toric charts indexed by stacky fans, with chart origins representing large-radius limit points.

proof_stages.label: Log mirror theorem and convergence
proof_stages.summary: The Coates-Corti-Iritani-Tseng theorem is stated: the completed big quantum connection of the toric stack X_sigma is isomorphic near the corresponding large-radius origin to the log Saito structure of the compactified mirror, with Poincare and higher-residue pairings matched and with an analytic-in-q, z-completed form needed for Stokes/Gamma arguments.

proof_stages.label: Critical-locus interpretation of toric transitions
proof_stages.summary: The spectrum of the logarithmic Jacobian ring over the compactified moduli is used to track how logarithmic critical points of W persist, collide, or escape across toric MMP wall crossings, thereby explaining the corresponding change in quantum cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Direct summand for discrepant flips
proof_stages.summary: For a discrepant toric flip, after z-completion the quantum connection of X_2 is a direct summand of the quantum connection of X_1, matching the expected semiorthogonal decomposition on derived categories and fitting the Gamma/Dubrovin conjectural picture.

examples.name: Projective-plane Hori-Vafa mirror
examples.note: The mirror of P^2 with potential W=x+y+q/(xy) is worked out to motivate the log compactification over q=0 and to show that the log Jacobian recovers the large-radius/classical cohomology limit.
examples.engagement_reason: he writes the P2 Hori–Vafa potential, derives it from fan monomials, identifies its Jacobian ring with quantum cohomology, and computes the logarithmic compactified Jacobian at q=0

passes.reason: the mirror of P^2 is the regular simplex toric Hori–Vafa potential with permutation symmetry, used only as an illustration
The mirror W=x+y+q/(xy) is the regular three-term/S3-symmetric toric Landau–Ginzburg mirror for P^2, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
S3-symmetric simplex Hori-Vafa mirror of P^2, the maximal-index toric del Pezzo, used only as a basic illustration.
the Hori-Vafa mirror of P^2 comes from the regular simplex fan and is used as a symmetric introductory model, not to refute a claim
The Hori–Vafa mirror of P^2 has the regular three-monomial/S3-symmetric simplex form and is used only as an illustrative toric mirror example.
The Hori–Vafa mirror of P2 is the regular simplex/three-term toric mirror used as a standard illustration, not to refute a claim.
Used as the basic toric mirror illustration, with the P^2 simplex fan giving a highly regular S3-symmetric Hori-Vafa potential.
It is used as a basic illustrative mirror, and the P^2 Hori–Vafa potential has the minimal simplex/three-monomial toric form with full permutation symmetry.
the mirror of P2 is the highly regular three-term simplex toric mirror used illustratively
The mirror is the regular three-term simplex potential for the highly symmetric maximal-index toric Fano surface P^2, used only illustratively.
It is the standard mirror of the maximally symmetric projective plane, an extremal del Pezzo/projective-space case, used only illustratively.
Used as a standard illustrative toric mirror, with the regular simplex/P^2 symmetry visible in the three-term potential.

examples.name: Crepant A1 resolution versus C^2/mu_2
examples.note: The three-point toric configuration giving Tot(O(-2) over P^1) and the orbifold C^2/mu_2 illustrates how two secondary-fan charts describe a crepant wall crossing and how the same number of critical branches appears on both sides.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws/describes the two A1 crepant-resolution fans, writes the corresponding mirror equation u1u2=q u3^2, and analyzes the resulting critical-point/Jacobian behavior

passes.reason: the A1 crepant resolution is a zero-discrepancy boundary case of the toric wall crossing, not an adversarial example
The A1 quotient/resolution is the minimal ADE crepant case and lies on the zero-discrepancy boundary, serving as a model example rather than a counterexample.
Minimal A1/ADE crepant resolution with one (-2)-curve and zero discrepancy, used as the crepant wall-crossing model.
the A1 crepant resolution is the zero-discrepancy boundary case of the toric birational comparison, used illustratively
The A1 crepant resolution is a zero-discrepancy boundary case of the toric wall crossing, presented illustratively rather than as a refutation.
The A1 crepant resolution is the zero-discrepancy boundary case of the toric wall crossing, used as the crepant model rather than as an obstruction.
Used to illustrate the crepant wall-crossing, and A1 is the rank-one minimal ADE case sitting on the discrepancy-zero boundary.
It is used as the standard crepant case; A1 is the minimal nontrivial ADE quotient singularity and the resolution is the zero-discrepancy boundary case, not a sporadic exception.
the A1 crepant resolution/orbifold pair is used as a standard local crepant toric wall-crossing example
The A1 resolution-to-quotient wall crossing is crepant, hence a zero-discrepancy boundary case, used as the model crepant example.
The A1 crepant resolution is the smallest ADE local model and a zero-discrepancy boundary case, presented as a standard example rather than a refutation.
Used to illustrate the crepant case, and the A1 resolution is a systematic discrepancy-zero boundary case rather than a sporadic exception.

examples.name: Blow-up of C^2 versus C^2
examples.note: The mirror family qu_3=u_1u_2 over P^1 illustrates a discrepant toric transition in which one critical branch disappears, matching the drop from the two-dimensional cohomology of the blow-up to the one-dimensional cohomology of C^2.
examples.engagement_reason: he gives the blow-up versus C2 fan picture, writes the mirror equation q u3=u1u2, and computes/compares the critical-point branches and cohomology dimensions

passes.reason: the blow-up of C^2 versus C^2 is a standard discrepant toric blow-down example illustrating the theorem, not refuting a claim
The blow-up of C^2 at one point is the basic one-exceptional-curve extremal contraction to a cohomologically trivial affine target, used to illustrate the discrepant case.
Elementary point blow-up of affine C^2 with one (-1)-curve and a trivial C^2 target, used as the discrepant wall-crossing model.
the point blow-up of C^2 versus C^2 is a standard non-crepant toric birational example, not an obstruction or refutation
The blow-up of C^2 at the origin versus C^2 is a standard non-crepant toric birational example used to show the expected critical-point behavior, not to disprove a claim.
The blow-up of C2 at the origin gives the basic Mori-extremal divisorial contraction to C2, illustrating the discrepant direct-summand case.
Used to illustrate the non-crepant toric wall-crossing, where the blow-down of the exceptional P1 is an elementary extremal divisorial contraction.
It is used as the standard discrepant case; blowing up the origin of C^2 is the elementary minimal divisorial contraction with the trivial affine plane as target.
the blow-up-to-affine-plane pair is used as a standard local discrepant toric example rather than to refute a claim
The blow-up/down of C^2 at the origin is the basic Mori extremal divisorial contraction, used as the standard discrepant example.
The blow-up of C^2 at the origin over C^2 is the elementary one-curve toric divisorial contraction/base discrepant model, not an adversarial example.
Used to illustrate the discrepant case, as the basic extremal blow-down of one exceptional curve to the contractible affine plane.
